Yes, it is possible to run different versions of your `create_app` function depending on whether you're running the Flask app as a server or executing a CLI command. However, the way you achieve this depends on how you structure your application and the commands you define. When using the app factory pattern, the `create_app` function is typically called once when the Flask app is initialized. But when you run a CLI command, you're not necessarily initializing the app in the same way as a server. This means you have more flexibility in how you handle command-line arguments and environment variables.

If you're running `flask my_command`, you can pass parameters directly to the CLI command without using environment variables. This can be done using the `@app.cli.command` decorator along with the `with_appcontext` decorator or by passing parameters directly to the command function. This allows you to distinguish between a CLI command and a server run without relying on environment variables.

You can pass parameters to the CLI command by using the `@app.cli.command` decorator with a `params` list. This gives you the ability to define command-line arguments that can be used to customize the behavior of your CLI command. These parameters can be used to determine whether to run a different `create_app` function or to pass additional configuration to the app.

If you want to avoid using environment variables, you can define a separate `create_app` function that is only used for CLI commands. This function can be called explicitly when the CLI command is executed, allowing you to pass different parameters or configurations. This approach keeps your code organized and avoids unnecessary complexity.

To pass parameters to the CLI command, you can use the `@app.cli.command` decorator with the `params` argument. This allows you to define arguments that are specific to the CLI command. These arguments can then be used to determine how the app should be initialized or configured. This is a clean and effective way to handle different scenarios without relying on environment variables.

You can use the `@app.cli.command` decorator with a `params` list to define multiple arguments for your CLI command. Each argument can be used to customize the behavior of the command. This makes it easy to pass different parameters when running the command, which can be used to trigger different logic or configurations.
